title: CEOs get AI twins as clones step up
full_text:
Australia’s chief executives are cloning themselves.
They have started using artificial intelligence to create digital twins to draft staff memos, triage diaries, and front investor calls, even in different languages.
It’s a trend that is upending the corporate affairs profession but which a leading headhunter says is yet to herald a full attack of the clones.
Xero chief executive Sukhinder Singh Cassidy, one of the ASX’s highest-paid executives, has built an AI version of her own voice on Anthropic’s Claude and sent it to her team so they can write as her.
The trans-Tasman accounting software titan’s boss is also using AI agents to better manage her calendar and, in her downtime, redesign her living room.
So-called “agentic AI” is driving the cloning – software that can act semi-autonomously without coding. It is the same wave of technology that has savaged the share prices of the software companies now scrambling to deploy it. Atlassian, WiseTech and even Xero have all been hit in what the industry has dubbed the “SaaSpocalypse”.
But Ms Singh Cassidy argues it’s better to adapt than resist. “I built a skill on (Claude) Cowork that’s called My Voice. And then I sent it to my team,” Ms Singh Cassidy told the Amazon Web Services AI summit this month, ahead of Xero’s full-year earnings. “I was like ‘OK, here’s a skill called Sukhinder’s Voice, please instead of rewriting now, can you guys just take this skill and get it to just help you write?’” A second agent called Daily Prep Tracker sweeps her calendar each afternoon. “So at one o’clock it fires off. It looks at all tomorrow’s meetings and it gives me a red, yellow or green on materials for the next meeting in my state of readiness,” she said.
Then there is the wardrobe.
“I love fashion. I upload images to ChatGPT multiple times a week and I’m like ‘OK, tell me what else to wear with this’. I have redesigned my living room multiple times, taking a picture … show me four different iterations of my living room.” Other executives to embrace AI include Ruslan Kogan, who has created a bot of himself to deliver company earnings.
WiseTech’s billionaire founder and executive chairman Richard White has also used the technology so seamlessly that he received a call from China, congratulating him on his fluency in Mandarin.
The only problem was Mr White can’t speak Chinese, or French, German or Spanish for that matter. But his AI avatar, which he unveiled in 2024, can.
The trend is disrupting the corporate affairs profession – a function that writes chief executive speeches, drafts media releases, handles investors, fronts government and runs crisis response when things go wrong.
But Temple Executive Search executive director and founder Rebecca Tabakoff said while cloned executives could now handle first drafts, the human touch was still essential.
“While developing an AI clone of senior executives can assist with first drafts of materials, corporate affairs teams will need to differentiate their leaders with artful impactful writing, long-term narrative development and judgement around audiences and timing,” Ms Tabakoff said.
“Poor use of AI will highlight the difference between effective writing and AI slop.” She said some of her clients were already running AI simulations of investors, regulators and customers to test board decisions before announcing them.
Companies must now also manage a new audience: the large language models themselves. Consumers are increasingly turning to ChatGPT, Claude and Google’s Gemini to pick products and services, forcing companies to defend their reputation inside the chatbots.
“Corporate affairs will need to return to old-fashioned PR and content strategies to ensure the LLMs are well informed and their reputation is safeguarded,” Ms Tabakoff said.
The same technology, she warned, is a gift to bad actors. “Companies need to prepare for an uplift in misinformation, frauds and scams against them,” she said.
